name: Nekst
classification_notes: Geometric sans-serif with display characteristics. Features alternate glyphs giving it a futuristic, constructivist quality. Described as looking like it is made from metal tubes at first glance, then revealing eccentric alternates.
summary: A geometric sans that appears deceptively simple until its eccentric alternates reveal a constructivist, futurist character.
description: Nekst reads as a clean geometric sans-serif at first glance, coarse and built from simple forms. The alternate glyphs change that impression dramatically, bringing a futuristic edge with hints of Bauhaus-era grotesques. Seven weights plus a variable font version make it versatile across both Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Nekst looks like a regular geometric sans, coarse and simple, as if made of metal tubes — until you activate the alternates, which bring an unexpected and eccentric futuristic taste with hints of old-time grotesques.

name: Displace 2.0
classification_notes: Display typeface described as digital calligraphy. Inspired by broad-nib writing and Roman mural lettering. Originally conceived for the logo of Mastactva magazine. Has a companion serif variant (Displace Serif).
summary: A display typeface rooted in broad-nib calligraphy and Roman mural lettering, spanning sans and serif variants.
description: Displace 2.0 sits at the intersection of classical broad-pen writing and contemporary digital design, drawing on ancient Roman letterforms. The family offers ten styles across five weights, each with roman and italic cuts. It gained recognition in European design circles and featured in the 56th Venice Biennale.
foundry_blurb: Displace 2.0 is a display font that belongs to several different type genres, but first and foremost it is digital calligraphy, inspired by broad-nib writing and the painted lettering of Roman murals.

name: Appetite Pro
classification_notes: Display serif with extensive ligatures, emerged from logo design work. The original 2011 release landed in MyFonts top 15 bestsellers. Revised and expanded in 2016 as Appetite Pro. Licensed for Lay's packaging in Russia (2020).
summary: Serebryakov's breakthrough display serif — a ligature-rich face born from logo work that reached the MyFonts bestseller list without promotion.
description: Appetite began as a logo typeface in 2011, organically reaching the top 15 on MyFonts before Serebryakov had promoted it at all. The expanded Pro version arrived in 2016 with a refined glyph set. The typeface has been licensed commercially for Lay's packaging in Russia.
foundry_blurb: Appetite Pro is Denis Serebryakov's most popular typeface, originally released in 2011 and later revised with an expanded character set and refined ligatures.

name: Canapa
classification_notes: Humanist sans-serif with a notable italic. Serebryakov has noted it awaits broader recognition.
summary: A humanist sans-serif from Serebryakov with a particularly considered italic, covering Latin and Cyrillic.
description: Canapa is a humanist sans-serif distinguished by its italic companion, which Serebryakov considers one of the stronger aspects of the design. Like all his typefaces, it supports both Latin and Cyrillic scripts.
foundry_blurb: Canapa is a humanist sans-serif with a notable italic version, designed for both Latin and Cyrillic text settings.

name: Epos
classification_notes: Condensed display typeface designed initially for Cyrillic, with Latin added later. Created for limited-edition Krinitsa beer packaging featuring Minsk urban legends. Three weights.
summary: A condensed display face born from Belarusian beer packaging, designed first in Cyrillic and expanded to Latin.
description: Epos was commissioned for Krinitsa beer packaging, a limited-edition series drawing on Minsk urban legends. Serebryakov designed the Cyrillic first and added Latin later, producing a condensed display typeface suited to packaging text with minimal hyphenation.
foundry_blurb: Epos was originally created for a limited-edition beer label featuring Minsk urban legends. The Cyrillic alphabet came first; Latin was added afterwards.
